sanskrit_devanagari:
रसोऽहमप्सु कौन्तेय प्रभास्मि शशिसूर्ययोः ।
प्रणवः सर्ववेदेषु शब्दः खे पौरुषं नृषु ॥ ८ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
raso 'ham apsu kaunteya prabhāsmi śaśi-sūryayoḥ |
praṇavaḥ sarva-vedeṣu śabdaḥ khe pauruṣaṁ nṛṣu || 8 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical).
exact (academic edition; text body identical; punctuation/danda rendering differs).
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_07.008 = MBh_06,029.008). Body text identical.

anvaya.meaning: of the moon and the sun

translations.verbatim_quote: O son of Kuntī, I am the taste of water, the light of the sun and the moon, the syllable oṁ in the Vedic mantras; I am the sound in ether and ability in man.
I am the taste in water, O son of Kunti, and the radiance of the sun and the moon. I am the sacred syllable Om in the Vedic mantras; I am the sound in ether, and the ability in humans.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: This verse explains how the Lord is all-pervasive by His diverse material and spiritual energies. The Supreme Lord can be preliminarily perceived by His different energies, and in this way He is realised.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 7.8 Kaunteya, O son of Kuntī, aham, I; am rasaḥ, the taste, which is the essence of water. The idea is that water is dependent on Me who am its essence. This is how it is to be understood in every case.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara gives the interpretive rule for the entire I-am-the-X-in-Y sequence: Krishna is named as the essence (sāra) of each thing, the property without which the thing would not be what it is. Water is dependent on the rasa-Krishna; the same logic applies to every member of the list.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 7.8 – 7.11 All these entities with their peculiar characteristic are born from Me alone. They depend on Me; inasmuch as they constitute My body, they exist in Me alone. Thus I alone exist while all of them are only My modes.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja treats 7.8-7.11 as a unit. The named properties are Krishna's modes (prakāras): each surface manifestation depends on him for its existence and for its peculiar characteristic. The Vishishtadvaita 'body of God' doctrine — every being is Krishna's mode — is invoked here.

positions.rendering: I am the taste of water (literal)
I am the taste in water (relational)
Krishna is the essence (sāra) — the property without which the thing would not be what it is; water depends on the rasa-Krishna
the named properties are Krishna's modes (prakāras); 7.8-7.11 form a unit; every entity exists as Krishna's body

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Translators differ on the preposition: 'taste of water' (genitive — Prabhupada) vs 'taste in water' (locative — Mukundananda). Sanskrit apsu is locative ('in waters'), so Mukundananda is more literal. Doctrinally: Shankara reads each I-am-X as identifying Krishna with the essence-property of the thing (the rasa is what makes water water); Ramanuja reads each X as a mode (prakāra) of Krishna — the property exists in the thing because Krishna sustains it. Engineering layers must hold both readings honestly: theistic identification (Krishna IS the property) is metaphysical and exceeds operational analog.

literal_meaning: I am the taste in waters, O son of Kuntī; I am the radiance in moon and sun; the praṇava (Oṁ) in all Vedas; sound in space; manhood in men.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The opening of the great I-am-the-X-in-Y sequence (7.8-7.11). Krishna names himself as the durable essence in each surface manifestation: the taste in water, the light in moon and sun, the syllable Oṁ in the Vedas, the sound in space, the manhood (capacity, ability) in men. Shankara reads each as essence (sāra) — the property without which the thing would not be what it is. Ramanuja reads each as Krishna's mode (prakāra) — the property exists because Krishna sustains it. Both traditions agree the verse is a theistic self-identification: Krishna is named, not merely the impersonal absolute is described.
